title: What Is Python
content: Python is a high-level programming language designed to emphasize readability and developer productivity. It is commonly used for web development, automation, data work, machine learning, and scripting.

title: Variables
content: A variable is a name that refers to a value in your program. Variables let you store data so you can reuse it, update it, and reason about your code more clearly.

title: Loops
content: A loop repeats a block of code. In Python, for loops are commonly used to iterate through items in a sequence, while while loops repeat as long as a condition stays true.

title: What Is A Function
content: A function is a reusable block of code that performs a specific task. Functions help reduce repetition, improve readability, and make programs easier to test.

title: Lists And List Comprehensions
content: A list stores an ordered collection of values. A list comprehension is a compact way to build a new list by iterating through an existing iterable and optionally filtering items. Use a normal loop when readability or multiple steps matter more than brevity.

title: For Loops Versus While Loops
content: Use a for loop when iterating over a known sequence such as a list, string, or range. Use a while loop when repetition depends on a condition that changes over time. Always make sure the while loop updates state so it can terminate.

title: Functions And Parameters
content: Functions package behavior into reusable blocks. Parameters are placeholders for input values. Prefer clear function names, focused responsibilities, and explicit return values so code is easier to test and reason about.

title: Debugging TypeError
content: TypeError usually means an operation was applied to the wrong kind of value. Inspect the actual runtime type with print or type(), then verify what each variable contains before the failing line. Beginners often mix strings and integers.

title: Dictionaries
content: A dictionary stores key-value pairs. It is useful when you need fast lookup by a meaningful key, such as mapping a username to profile data. Access values with square brackets or use get() when a missing key is possible.

title: Writing Clean Beginner Python
content: Write code that is easy to read first. Use descriptive variable names, avoid deeply nested logic, break problems into functions, and test small pieces independently. Simple code is easier to debug than clever code.

title: Conditionals
content: Conditionals let a program make decisions. In Python, if, elif, and else are used to run different blocks of code depending on whether a condition is true or false.

title: Classes
content: A class is a blueprint for creating objects. Classes let related data and behavior stay grouped together, which is useful for organizing larger programs.

title: Exceptions
content: An exception is an error that happens while a program is running. Python can raise exceptions when something goes wrong, and try/except blocks can be used to handle them more safely.

title: Modules
content: A module is a Python file that contains code such as functions, variables, or classes that can be imported into another file. Modules help organize code and make it reusable.

title: File Handling
content: File handling is the process of reading from or writing to files. In Python, files are commonly opened with open(), and using a with statement helps ensure they are closed properly.

title: Strings
content: A string is a sequence of text characters. Strings are used for things like names, messages, and user input, and Python provides many built-in methods for working with them.

title: Tuples
content: A tuple is an ordered collection of values, similar to a list, but tuples are immutable. They are useful when a group of values should not change.

title: Sets
content: A set is an unordered collection of unique values. Sets are useful when duplicates should be removed or when checking membership efficiently.
